info.text: In this demo I’ve imported Hydra, a web-based visual synth for the browser inspired by analog video systems and live-coding. Created by the artist Olivia Jack and others, Hydra lets you compose graphic shaders (WebGL visuals) by chaining functions (oscillators, shapes, color ops, blends) in JavaScript.
info.title: getting started
live coding
info.text: Hydra is often used to create realtime visuals in "live coding" contexts like algoraves, which is why Olivia also created an online hydra editor. In this example we're importing hydra as a stand-alone library, which allows us to incorporate it into our web projects and combine it with other creative libraries.

info.title: canvas
info.text: We start in a setup function by creating a canvas element and setting its size to 600 by 600 (or whatever size you want).

info.title: hydra instance
info.text: Then we make a new Hydra instance and point it at that canvas. Setting detectAudio to false means we’re not asking for microphone access. However if you want to, Hydra can pull data from the mic and camera and send live audio/video into the synth.

info.title: osc
info.text: With hydra imported we can build a signal chain, we'll start with an osc() which creates a striped oscillator pattern: the first number is spatial frequency (how many stripes), the second is the rate of change over time, and the third shifts the phase.

info.title: color
info.text: We'll chain a couple more Hydra methods to our signal. Next the .color() tints the output of our osc toward a warmer yellow using normalized RGB (red, green, blue) values (0–1). Lastly, .out() sends the current signal graph to the Hydra output (our canvas).

info.title: hydra flow
info.text: That’s the Hydra flow in a nutshell: define sources, transform them with chained operators, and route the output to a canvas. We could process our signal even more using other Hydra methods simply by adding others to the mix, you can get some very powerful visuals all in a few compact lines of code.

info.title: append canvas
info.text: At the end of our setup we need to make sure to add the hydra.canvas to the page so the rendered result is visible.

info.title: on load
info.text: As with many of our other demos, the last line in our code sets everything going by running our setup function as soon as our page has loaded.

info.title: lots more
info.text: There’s loads more you can do with this library—this is only the beginning. Hydra has interactive documentation, stacks of chainable functions (mixing, modulating, kaleidoscopes, pixelation, feedback loops), and even a browser-based live coding editor. You can route video inputs, use screen capture as a source, and sync visuals to audio you create with other APIs/libraries for reactive pieces.
